senses.definition: used to refer to something that is inexplicably difficult

examples.x: Convincing him to try a new hobby was like pulling teeth.
Getting a straight answer from the evasive witness during the interrogation was like pulling teeth.
Trying to gather accurate data for the research project was like pulling teeth.
Getting my teenage son to clean his room is like pulling teeth.
Persuading him to lift the charges was a challenge, like pulling teeth.

senses.definition: problems that a person encounters in the early stages of a project or activity

examples.x: There were the usual teething troubles at the beginning of the project, but that's to be expected.
We had some teething problems when we first opened the bookshop, but now everything is okay.

senses.definition: said of something that is very complex or difficult for one understands or work with
(of a task) to be very difficult or almost impossible to do or complete

examples.x: Trying to assemble the complicated furniture without the instructions was murder.
Dealing with a difficult boss can be murder.
The long hours and physical labor required for the construction project were murder on the workers.
Balancing work and family commitments can be murder.
Learning a new language from scratch can be murder.

senses.definition: a difficult fight or challenge that requires a lot of effort and determination

examples.x: Equip yourself with five practical strategies that help you win the uphill battle and take your project professional career to new heights.
Starting a new business in a competitive market is an uphill battle.
The young athlete faced an uphill battle to recover from a serious injury and get back into peak form for the upcoming championship.
Advocates for the policy reform faced an uphill battle as they encountered opposition from powerful interest groups and entrenched beliefs.
Breaking into the entertainment industry can be an uphill battle for aspiring actors.

senses.definition: a task or challenge that requires an immense amount of effort, strength, or endurance to accomplish

examples.x: Organizing the event within a tight deadline was a Herculean task, requiring round-the-clock work and coordination.
Repairing the dilapidated old house proved to be a Herculean task for the homeowners.
The teacher faced the Herculean task of keeping a classroom full of energetic kindergarteners engaged and focused throughout the day.
Launching a successful startup company from scratch is a Herculean task.
Convincing the board of directors to approve the ambitious project was a Herculean task.

senses.definition: a difficult and challenging situation or task that requires significant effort to overcome or complete

examples.x: The dense academic textbook was heavy going, requiring multiple readings and extensive note-taking to fully understand the complex concepts.
Running a marathon in extreme weather conditions can be heavy going.
The negotiations between the two parties were heavy going, with both sides struggling to find common ground and reach a mutually beneficial agreement.
The hiker found the steep, rocky trail to be heavy going.
The intricate puzzle proved to be heavy going, with its intricate design and numerous pieces requiring intense concentration and problem-solving skills.

word_list.pronunciation: ɐ tˈʌf hˈɑːɹd ɹˈoʊ tə hˈoʊ

senses.definition: a situation that causes difficulties for one

examples.x: Tax reform was one of the new president's primary campaign promises, but it will likely prove a hard row to hoe, given the deep divisions in congress.
Starting a new business without any financial backing is a tough row to hoe.
Taking care of an aging parent with complex medical needs can be a hard row to hoe.
Training for a marathon is a tough row to hoe, requiring months of dedicated preparation, discipline, and pushing through physical and mental barriers.
Climbing the corporate ladder in a competitive industry can be a hard row to hoe.

word_list.pronunciation: bˈaɪt ˈɔf mˈoːɹ ðɐn ˌɛsbˈiː kæn kʊd tʃjˈuː

senses.definition: to attempt to do something that is beyond one's capability

examples.x: They bit off more than they could chew trying to organize the event without enough resources.
He warned her not to bite off more than she could chew with such an ambitious goal.
Don't bite off more than you can chew by promising to finish the task in one day.
He bit off more than he could chew by agreeing to lead two projects at once.
By taking on the job and planning a wedding simultaneously, she clearly bit off more than she could chew.

word_list.pronunciation: æz ɔːɹ ɹˈɛd æz ɐ tʃˈɛɹi

senses.definition: turning really red in the face because of doing a lot of physical activities

examples.x: My father was as red as a cherry by the time we reached the top of the hill.

senses.definition: used to describe a job or task that is too difficult or challenging

examples.x: Getting a group of young children to sit still and listen can be like herding cats.
Trying to manage the logistics of a large project with multiple teams and conflicting priorities can feel like herding cats.
Organizing a family gathering with relatives who have different schedules and preferences can be like herding cats.
Attempting to coordinate a group of inexperienced volunteers without clear instructions can be like herding cats.
Managing a team of creative individuals who have strong opinions and different working styles can feel like herding cats.

senses.definition: the hardest or most boring part of a task or job
a situation that is very difficult because one is not prepared to deal with it

examples.x: I'm a little nervous about starting my graduate degree program, but I'm determined to jump in at the deep end and give it a go.
When I started my new job, they threw me in at the deep end by assigning me a major project on my first day.
John was pushed into the deep end of parenthood when his wife unexpectedly had to travel for work, leaving him to care for their three young children.
The new manager was thrown in at the deep end, having to handle a crisis situation within her first week on the job.
I decided to take the plunge and sign up for an advanced language course, knowing it would throw me in at the deep end, but I was eager to challenge myself.

senses.definition: to utilize one's final and decisive efforts, often in a critical moment

examples.x: He started off the game well but seemed to have shot his bolt by half-time.
The team had been trailing behind, but in the final minutes, they shot their bolt and managed to score two goals, securing a surprising victory.
The politician had been saving his most powerful arguments for the end of the debate, and when his turn came, he shot his bolt, delivering a passionate speech that resonated with the audience.
The company had been struggling financially, and they decided to shoot their bolt by launching a bold marketing campaign to revive their brand.
After months of preparation, the athlete shot her bolt in the Olympic finals, giving her best performance but narrowly missing the gold medal.
